content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

July 31, 2006

Cynthia Murray
495 Willow Court
Novato, CA 94945


RE: Your Request for Informal Assistance
     Our File No. I-06-135

Dear Ms. Murray:

     This letter is in response to your request regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Since you have not identified a specific decision to be made as a public official, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance pursuant to Regulation 18329(c).² Please note that this advice is limited to the provisions of the Act. Other laws may apply, e.g., laws relating to "incompatible offices." You should ask your county counsel for advice regarding this and other laws that may apply.

QUESTION

     As a Marin County Supervisor, is your employment as a part-time Interim Executive Director for the North Bay Council a conflict of interest or otherwise prohibited by the Act?

CONCLUSION

     There is nothing in the Act that prohibits outside employment by public officials. However, the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions prohibit a public official from making, participating in making, or attempting to use his or her official position to influence a governmental decision if the decision will have a material financial effect on the official's personal financial interests, as discussed below.


¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.
² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or written advice. (Section 83114; regulation 18329(c)(3) copy enclosed.)

File No. I-06-135
Page No. 2

FACTS

You are on the Marin County Board of Supervisors serving the last six months of
your second term. In anticipation of leaving office at the end of this year, you are
considering starting a consulting business.³

You have accepted a three-month consulting contract with the North Bay Council,
a non-profit business advocacy group with approximately 20 members in Marin and
Sonoma counties. In this period, you will serve as the Interim Executive Director. Your
hours will be part-time, no more than 20 hours per week.

The North Bay Council is fully funded by its members, receives no funding from
the County of Marin, rarely lobbies the board of supervisors, and has no items that would
come before the board for approval. You intend to recuse yourself if there is any
lobbying by the North Bay Council or its members.

DISCUSSION

The Act was adopted by the voters of California in 1974. The purpose for the
conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act was to ensure that public officials, whether
elected or appointed, would perform their duties in an impartial manner, free from any
bias caused by their own financial interests or the financial interests of persons who have
supported them. (Section 81001(b).)

In furtherance of this goal, section 87100 of the Act prohibits any public official
from making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her official position to
influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. The Act
does not ban private employment while being a public official.

The Commission uses an eight-step process in determining whether a public
official has a disqualifying conflict of interest in a particular governmental decision.
(Regulation 18700, subdivisions (b)(1) - (8).) Without a specific governmental decision,
we are unable to perform the analysis to determine if a conflict of interest exists.
However, the following review of the eight-step process may be used to offer guidance if
a possible conflict arises.

Steps 1 & 2: Are you a public official making, participating in making, or influencing a
governmental decision?

As an elected member of the Marin County Board of Supervisors, you are a
public official under the Act. (Section 82048.) Consequently, you may not make,
participate in making, or otherwise use your official position to influence any decision

³ Although you state that you are considering starting a consulting business, you also state that you
have already accepted a three-month consulting contract with the North Bay Council. The Commission
will not advise with respect to past conduct. (Regulation 18329(b)(8)(A).) Therefore, nothing in this letter
should be construed to evaluate any conduct that may have already taken place.

File No. I-06-135
Page No. 3

that will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on any economic interest
you may have.

You "make a governmental decision" when you, acting within the authority of
your office or position, vote on a matter, obligate or commit your agency to any course of
action, or enter into any contractual agreement on behalf of yourself. (Regulation
18702.1.) You "participate in making a governmental decision" when, acting within the
authority of your position and without significant substantive or intervening review, you
negotiate, advise, or make recommendations to the decision maker regarding the
governmental decision. (Regulation 18702.2.) You attempt to use your official position
to "influence" a decision if, for the purpose of influencing, you contact or appear before
any member, officer, employee, or consultant of your agency. (Regulation 18702.3.)

Step 3: What are your economic interests?

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to conflicts of interest arising
from economic interests. These economic interests are defined in section 87103 of the
Act and regulations 18703.1 - 18703.5, summarized below:

* A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she
has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more (section 87103(a); regulation
18703.1(a)); or in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee,
or holds any position of management (section 87103(d); regulation 18703.1(b));

* A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she
has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more (section 87103(b); regulation
18703.2);

* A public official has an economic interest in any source of income, including
promised income,⁴ which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to
the time in which the relevant governmental decision was made (section
87103(c); regulation 18703.3);

* A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if
the gifts aggregate to $360 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (section
87103(e); regulation 18703.4);

* A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal finances and
those of his or her immediate family - this is the "personal financial effects" rule
(section 87103; regulation 18703.5).

You stated that you are considering starting a consulting business and have
accepted a three-month consulting contract with the North Bay Council. Assuming your

⁴ A public official's income includes income that has been promised to the public official, but not
yet received by him or her, if he or she has a legally enforceable right to the promised income. (Regulation
18703.3(a)(1).)

File No. I-06-135
Page No. 4

ownership interest is worth $2,000 or more, your consulting business will be an
investment interest of yours. (Section 87103(a) and (d).) In addition, we assume that
North Bay Council will be a source of income to you of $500 or more. (Section
87103(c).)

In addition, you always have an economic interest in your personal finances, and
may have a conflict of interest in any decision foreseeably resulting in an increase or
decrease in your personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities or those of your
immediate family, in the amount of $250 or more over a 12-month period. (Regulation
18703.5.)

For steps four through eight, a specific governmental decision is required to
continue with the analysis. However, to provide a general discussion, the remaining steps
involve the following:

Step 4: For each of your economic interests, determine whether that interest is directly
or indirectly involved in the governmental decision that you will be making, participating
in making, or using or attempting to use your official position to influence. (See
regulation 18704.)

Step 5: Determine the applicable materiality standard for each economic interest, based
upon the degree of involvement determined pursuant to regulation 18704. (See
regulation 18705.)

Step 6: Determine whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision
will have a material financial effect on your economic interests. If it is not reasonably
foreseeable that there will be such an effect on any of your economic interests, you do not
have a conflict of interest within the meaning of the Act. (See regulation 18707.)

Step 7: Determine if the reasonably foreseeable financial effect is distinguishable from
the effect on the public generally. If you can establish that such an effect on your
economic interest is indistinguishable from the effect on the public generally, you do not
have a conflict of interest within the meaning of the Act. (See regulation 18707.)

Step 8: Determine if your participation is legally required despite your conflict of
interest. (See regulation 18708.)

Also, according to your facts, you are serving the last six months of your term.
As of July 1, 2006, local elected officials and others are prohibited from making certain
appearances before their former agency for one year. (See section 87406.3.) Should
your new employment require you, for compensation, to appear before the Board of
Supervisors once you leave office, you should seek additional advice.

File No. 1-06-135
Page No. 5

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

By: Kevin S. Moen, PhD
Political Reform Consultant II
Technical Assistance Division

governmental decision if the decision will have a material financial effect on the

2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or
formal written advice. (Section 831 14; regulation 18329(c)(3) copy enclosed.)
